event: Madonna and Child with the Infant Saint John the Baptist
symbols: Virgin Mary in blue and red — the union of heavenly purity (blue) and sacrificial love (red) in the Mother of God
Christ Child held close to Mary — the Incarnation, God made vulnerable flesh in human arms
young John the Baptist with cross-staff — the forerunner who will proclaim Christ as the Lamb of God
landscape vista behind — the world into which Christ comes as Savior
architectural column at left — the Church, the pillar of truth, framing the holy scene
description: The Virgin Mary, wearing a dark blue mantle over a red dress with a white veil, holds the plump Infant Jesus on her lap while looking down tenderly. The Christ Child gazes outward at the viewer with a lively expression. At the lower left, the young John the Baptist, with curly reddish hair, holds a thin cross-staff and looks up at Jesus. Behind the figures, a soft Italian landscape with distant blue mountains and sky opens up. A stone column or architectural element frames the left edge of the composition. The painting exemplifies Correggio's sfumato technique and warm, gentle palette.
theological_description: Correggio presents the classic devotional subject of the Virgin and Child accompanied by the infant John the Baptist. The theological heart of the image is the Incarnation: the eternal Word of God (JHN.1.1) held as a helpless infant in the arms of his human mother. Mary's dual colors — blue for heaven, red for earth and sacrifice — visually express the doctrine of the Theotokos: she is the meeting point of divine and human natures. The young Baptist, traditionally identified by his cross-staff, is the forerunner whose entire existence points toward Christ. Even as a child, John is depicted turning toward Jesus, prefiguring his adult proclamation: 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!' (JHN.1.29). The cross-staff anticipates both Christ's Passion and John's own martyrdom. Luke's Gospel records the connection between these two children from before birth — when the pregnant Mary visited Elizabeth, the baby John leaped in the womb at the presence of the Lord (LUK.1.41-44). Mary's tender downward gaze embodies the Magnificat's spirit of humble gratitude: 'My soul magnifies the Lord' (LUK.1.46). The open landscape suggests the world that awaits the ministry of both figures — one as herald, the other as Redeemer.

scripture_refs.reason: The Word became flesh and dwelt among us — the Incarnation depicted in the Christ Child
John declaring Jesus the Lamb of God — prefigured in the infant Baptist gazing at Christ
The infant John leaping in Elizabeth's womb at the presence of Christ — the childhood meeting echoes this
Mary's Magnificat — her tender maternal gaze reflecting humble gratitude and praise
John as the voice crying in the wilderness, preparing the way of the Lord
The virgin shall conceive and bear a son — the prophetic foundation of the Madonna and Child motif
